pages.title: Brew-day pH control
pages.excerpt: Two-checkpoint brew-day protocol: hit 5.2-5.4 in the mash at 15 minutes in, hit 5.5-5.8 in the sparge water before runoff.

pages.title: Brew-day timeline
pages.excerpt: Clock-relative timeline for a brew day on the BrewZilla 35L + FermZilla rig: what's active at each block and where the schedule shifts for different styles.

pages.title: Building a brewing water profile
pages.excerpt: Cheat-sheet for picking a target ion profile from the style and reading off salt additions from your starting-water row.

pages.title: Classifying a beer
pages.excerpt: How to place a beer in its style family by reading five things in order: fermentation type, yeast character, malt depth, hop intensity, and vital stats.

pages.title: The science of brewing
pages.excerpt: Master index from brewing-science questions to the concept pages that carry the mechanism: six clusters spanning mash, boil, hop, yeast, water, and conditioning.

pages.title: Auditing an instruction artifact
pages.excerpt: When an LLM instruction artifact has grown across revisions and feels noisy, but no specific rule has failed yet.

pages.title: Authoring demonstrations
pages.excerpt: When you've decided a demonstration is the right tool and need to write the examples that pin a demonstrable boundary.

pages.title: Authoring frames
pages.excerpt: When you've decided a frame is the right tool and need to pick the framing axis and write the move.

pages.title: Authoring rules
pages.excerpt: When typing a rule into an LLM instruction artifact and you want it to recruit the intended behavior on first read.

pages.title: Choosing the right instruction
pages.excerpt: Deciding which modes — rule, frame, demo, or combination — the target behavior calls for.

pages.title: Composing instructions
pages.excerpt: When a prompt already carries at least one move and you're about to add another, or when quality has plateaued under accumulated moves.

pages.title: Designing a subagent dispatch boundary
pages.excerpt: When dispatching subtask work to a subagent and you want the boundary to hold without leaking working material into the parent's context.

pages.title: Diagnosing a silent compliance failure
pages.excerpt: When an LLM directive looks correctly written, the model has read it, and behavior diverged from intent without raising any error.

pages.title: Encoding reasoning structure
pages.excerpt: When an instruction requires multi-step judgment, encode reasoning structure so the model thinks before it commits.

pages.title: Preventing hallucination
pages.excerpt: When an instruction requires factual claims or specifics, structure it so the model surfaces gaps instead of fabricating through them.

pages.title: Reading model misbehavior
pages.excerpt: When an instruction artifact produces unexpected output, read the model's behavior to identify which mechanism misfired.

pages.title: Vetting context construction
pages.excerpt: When you've finished writing an LLM-facing instruction system and want to vet how it uses the context window before shipping.

pages.title: Reference map
pages.excerpt: When sitting down to write, revise, or debug an LLM instruction artifact and not sure which reference to open first.

pages.title: Writing trigger and output contracts
pages.excerpt: When authoring metadata that lives in a contract with other system layers, not as self-contained directives.

mottos.text: Rules constrain. Frames recruit. Demos pin boundaries.
Every instruction you add dilutes every instruction you already have.
The model didn't refuse — it quietly did something else.
Name the identity. The behavior follows.
What you left out of the window matters as much as what you put in.
If the model can't cite it, make it say so.
Think before you commit — and make the model do the same.
The beer starts in the water report.
Taste before you type the style.
The clock runs the brew day, not the recipe.
Every grain tells you what it wants — if you measure.
